---
title: "Module 1 — Mathematical Foundations"
subtitle: "Prerequisites and core tools for linear control theory"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
---

This module covers the mathematical prerequisites for the rest of the course.
Every later chapter draws on at least one of these foundations, so the material is developed with enough depth to make the connections to control theory explicit.

## Chapters

| # | Title | Key Topics |
|---|---|---|
| 1.1 | [Linear Algebra](1.1_linear_algebra.md) | Vector spaces, eigenvalues, Jordan form, inner products, norms |
| 1.2 | [Matrix Factorizations](1.2_matrix_factorizations.md) | LU, Cholesky, QR, SVD, Schur complement |
| 1.3 | Ordinary Differential Equations | Matrix exponential, linear ODE systems, difference equations |
| 1.4 | Numerical Methods | Conditioning, Gaussian elimination, ODE solvers |
| 1.5 | Probability & Statistics | Gaussian distribution, covariance, expectation |
| 1.6 | Convexity Basics | Convex sets, convex functions, first/second-order conditions |

## Prerequisites

No prior control knowledge is assumed. Familiarity with calculus and introductory linear algebra (matrix multiplication, determinants) is helpful but not required.

## How These Topics Connect to Control

- **1.1 + 1.2** form the algebraic and numerical foundation used in every subsequent chapter.
- **1.3** develops the time-domain solution of state-space systems (Chapter 3).
- **1.4** bridges theory and simulation, needed for implementing any control algorithm.
- **1.5** enables stochastic models, Kalman filtering (Chapter 10), and system identification (Chapter 9).
- **1.6** connects directly to optimization (Chapter 2) and Lyapunov-based synthesis (Chapter 12).
